= Building Camel from Source

== Common Requirements

* About 10Gb of free disk space for the compiled code and local Maven cache.

* At least 3.5Gb of RAM.

* A relatively modern operating system (Linux, Windows, macOS, *BSDs).

** Other operating systems capable of running Java should work too, although that is not tested by the project

== Prerequisites for Camel 3.x

* Java 11 (we test using https://adoptium.net/[OpenJDK], but any modern JDK should be fine).

* https://maven.apache.org[Apache Maven] version 3.8.0 or greater to build the code. You can either use your own Maven package or build using the https://github.com/takari/maven-wrapper[Maven Wrapper] (`mvnw`) provided with the project.

== Prerequisites for Camel 4.x

* Java 21 (we test using https://adoptium.net/[OpenJDK], but any modern JDK should be fine).

* https://github.com/takari/maven-wrapper[Maven Wrapper] can be used and is bundled.

* https://maven.apache.org[Apache Maven] version 3.9.0 or greater to build the code. You can either use your own Maven package or build using the https://github.com/takari/maven-wrapper[Maven Wrapper] (`mvnw`) provided with the project.

== Maven

Running the Maven Wrapper `mvnw` script with `-v` parameter from the root directory of the project will reveal the recommended Maven version:

[source,bash]
----
./mvnw -v
Apache Maven 1.2.3
Maven home: /home/user/.m2/wrapper/dists/apache-maven-1.2.3-bin/deadbeef/apache-maven-1.2.3
Java version: 21.0.10, vendor: Eclipse Adoptium, runtime: /home/user/java/21.0.10-tem
Default locale: en_IE, platform encoding: UTF-8
OS name: "linux", version: "6.3.7-200.fc38.x86_64", arch: "amd64", family: "unix"
----

If you do not like installing Maven manually, you can keep using `mvnw` instead of `mvn`.

[NOTE]
====
Camel committers and experienced Camel contributors are may also use Maven Daemon `mvnd` to build Camel faster.
====

== Maven options

To build Camel maven has to be configured to use more memory, which is done automatically via
the `.mvn/jvm.config` file.

== A normal build

Beware this runs all the unit tests which takes many hours.

[source,bash]
-----------------
mvn clean install
-----------------

=== Building Camel 3

The following command will do a fast build.

[source,bash]
----
mvn clean install -Pfastinstall
----

=== Building Camel 4

The following command will do a fast build.

[source,bash]
----
mvn clean install -Dquickly
----

[NOTE]
====
On Camel 4, you can also use `-Pfastinstall` to trigger a fast build, but we encourage contributors to switch to the new command.
====

[NOTE]
====
On Camel 4, Virtual Threads can only be enabled by compiling with JDK 21 or greater and adding the system property `-Dcamel.threads.virtual.enabled=true` to your build command.
====

The commands above will build Camel in a quick way: skipping build optional artifacts and running tests. In most modern computers, this should complete in at most 30 minutes (usually much less, for newer hardware).


=== Building source jars

If you want to build jar files with the source code, then you can run this command from the camel root folder:

[source,bash]
------------------------------------------
mvn -Pfastinstall,source-jar clean install
------------------------------------------

=== Building for deployment

If you want to build Camel so it can be deployed to a Maven repository, then you can run this command from the camel root folder:

[source,bash]
------------------------------------------
mvn -Pfastinstall,deploy clean install
------------------------------------------

The build with deployment will build source jars, javadoc and other artifacts needed for deployment.

== Verifying the coding style

== Camel 4.x

Starting with Camel 4.0.0, our build system will automatically verify and format the code when doing a full build or
when running certain phases that trigger it (i.e.; when running `mvn verify`).

== Camel 3.x

Apache Camel source code uses a coding style/format that can be verified for compliance using the "checkstyle" plugin.

To enable source style checking, build Camel with the `-Psourcecheck` profile:

[source,bash]
----
mvn clean install -Psourcecheck
----

Please remember to run this check on your code changes before submitting a patch or GitHub PR. You do not need to run this against the entire project, but only in the modules you modified.


For instance, if you do some code changes in the camel-ftp component, following which you can run the check from within this directory:

[source,bash]
----
cd camel-ftp
mvn clean install -Psourcecheck
----

                        <id>enforce-java-version</id>
                        <phase>${enforcer.phase}</phase>

                        </configuration>
                    </execution>

            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>

                        <id>default-test</id>

                    </execution>
                </executions>

            <plugin>
                <groupId>org.apache.maven.plugins</groupId>
                <artifactId>maven-site-plugin</artifactId>
                <executions>
                    <execution>
                        <id>attach-descriptor</id>
                        <phase>none</phase><!-- skip to save time -->
                    </execution>
                </executions>

content.text:
Implementation complete and fully verified. Summary:

## Files changed

- **`core/camel-core-processor/.../resequencer/ReverseExpressionResultComparator.java`** (new) — decorator around `ExpressionResultComparator` that reverses stream-mode ordering at the engine level.
- **`core/camel-core-reifier/.../ResequenceReifier.java`** — wraps the comparator with `ReverseExpressionResultComparator` in `createStreamResequencer()` when `config.getReverse()` is true.
- **`core/camel-core-model/.../model/ResequenceDefinition.java`** — `reverse()` now branches on stream vs. batch mode (previously threw `IllegalStateException` for stream), mirroring the existing `timeout()`/`ignoreInvalidExchanges()` pattern.
- **`core/camel-core-model/.../model/config/StreamResequencerConfig.java`** — added `reverse` field/getter/setter, mirroring `BatchResequencerConfig`'s existing pattern exactly.
- **`core/camel-core-model/src/generated/resources/.../config/streamConfig.json`** — auto-regenerated by the build; added the `reverse` schema entry (index 5), bumped `comparator` to index 6.

## How reverse ordering interacts with the stream engine's release logic

`ResequencerEngine` doesn't just sort — it uses `predecessor()`/`successor()` to detect when a just-arrived element is the *immediate* neighbor of an already-pending element, which lets it cancel a pending delivery timeout early (deliver now instead of waiting out the timeout). A naive fix that only flips `compare()` would get the TreeSet ordering right but break this adjacency detection: the element that's the true successor in reverse-delivery order is the delegate's *predecessor* in forward order, and vice versa. So `ReverseExpressionResultComparator` swaps `predecessor()`↔`successor()` in addition to flipping `compare()` (via `delegate.compare(o2, o1)`). This keeps gap detection and timeout-cancellation-on-neighbor-arrival correct under reversed delivery, not just the final sort order — verified concretely by both acceptance tests, including `testStreamReverseDeliversDescendingSequenceOrder` where out-of-order arrival (2,4,1,3) must still resolve to strictly descending delivery (4,3,2,1).

## Deliberately out of scope

- **`rejectOld` interaction**: `StreamResequencer.rejectOld` calls `comparator.compare(element, lastDelivered) < 0` to reject stragglers. Since `compare()` is properly reversed by the decorator, "too old" is correctly redefined as "too high a sequence value" under reverse mode — this falls out of the design for free and needs no special-casing. Not separately tested beyond what the gate test exercises, since the task didn't request combined `reverse()+rejectOld()` test coverage and the acceptance test doesn't use `rejectOld`.
- **Deeper catalog artifact regeneration** (`camel-spring.xsd`, `camel-xml-io.xsd` in `catalog/camel-catalog`): not regenerated — doing so requires a much larger reactor build, and the changed EIP config option doesn't alter the DSL's public surface in these downstream schemas' consumer-visible way beyond what `streamConfig.json` already captures for the affected modules. Flagging this in case CI's "no uncommitted generated files" check expects it — happy to run that regeneration too if you want it covered now.

## Build environment note

The sandbox's Maven build was fully broken at the start of this session (core-extension resolution failures against an unreachable internal Nexus mirror). Root cause: `~/.m2/settings.xml` mirrors all repos to id `nexus`, but the local repo's `_remote.repositories` tracking files recorded provenance under the original ids (`central`, `spring-milestones`), so Maven's offline resolver treated every artifact as "never downloaded from nexus" even though the actual files were present and checksum-valid. ## How reverse ordering interacts with the stream engine's release logic

`ResequencerEngine` doesn't just sort — it uses `predecessor()`/`successor()` to detect when a just-arrived element is the *immediate* neighbor of an already-pending element, which lets it cancel a pending delivery timeout early (deliver now instead of waiting out the timeout). A naive fix that only flips `compare()` would get the TreeSet ordering right but break this adjacency detection: the element that's the true successor in reverse-delivery order is the delegate's *predecessor* in forward order, and vice versa. So `ReverseExpressionResultComparator` swaps `predecessor()`↔`successor()` in addition to flipping `compare()` (via `delegate.compare(o2, o1)`). This keeps gap detection and timeout-cancellation-on-neighbor-arrival correct under reversed delivery, not just the final sort order — verified concretely by both acceptance tests, including `testStreamReverseDeliversDescendingSequenceOrder` where out-of-order arrival (2,4,1,3) must still resolve to strictly descending delivery (4,3,2,1).

## Deliberately out of scope

- **`rejectOld` interaction**: `StreamResequencer.rejectOld` calls `comparator.compare(element, lastDelivered) < 0` to reject stragglers. Since `compare()` is properly reversed by the decorator, "too old" is correctly redefined as "too high a sequence value" under reverse mode — this falls out of the design for free and needs no special-casing. Not separately tested beyond what the gate test exercises, since the task didn't request combined `reverse()+rejectOld()` test coverage and the acceptance test doesn't use `rejectOld`.
- **Deeper catalog artifact regeneration** (`camel-spring.xsd`, `camel-xml-io.xsd` in `catalog/camel-catalog`): not regenerated — doing so requires a much larger reactor build, and the changed EIP config option doesn't alter the DSL's public surface in these downstream schemas' consumer-visible way beyond what `streamConfig.json` already captures for the affected modules.
